claim: Three behaviorally distinct lost dog profiles require different recovery strategies: Gregarious (friendly, self-adoption risk near escape point), Aloof (wary but eventually accepts contact when hungry, often found weeks/months later), and Skittish (travels farthest, highest car-strike risk, likely to be misidentified as abused or feral).
stance_target: Behavioral profile is the primary stratification variable for lost dog search protocol
scope_conditions: All lost dog scenarios; foundational MAR typology by Kat Albrecht
quoted_support: Gregarious (wiggly-butt, friendly dogs)... at risk of self-adoption. Aloof temperaments are wary of strangers... often not recovered for weeks or months. Skittish temperaments... more inclined to travel farther and are at a higher risk of being hit by cars.

claim: The olfactory portion of a dog's brain shuts down during fight-or-flight; a panicked dog will not recognize its owner's scent, so owners should not rely on scent recognition during an encounter with their scared lost dog.
stance_target: Calling or approaching a panicked dog — even by the owner — is counterproductive
scope_conditions: Fight-or-flight state; applies to all skittish and traumatized dogs during active flight
quoted_support: the olfactory portion of a dog's brain closes down during the 'fight or flight' process and that a panicked dog likely won't recognize their guardian's scent. Guardians should be prepared that their timid lost dog may run from them.

claim: Offering cash rewards for lost dogs creates two problems: scamming (false claims for reward money) and chasing (would-be rescuers rush to sightings and chase the dog, sometimes into traffic).
stance_target: Reward offers increase risk of secondary harm; 'PLEASE HELP FIND' appeal to altruism is preferable
scope_conditions: Social media and public poster campaigns in lost dog cases
quoted_support: rewards frequently create two problems: scamming and chasing. Chasing occurs when would-be-rescuers who really want to help recover a lost pet will rush to the area where there was a sighting of the dog and ultimately ending up chasing the dog, tragically sometimes resulting in the dog being killed in traffic.

claim: The 'house as trap' technique captures skittish dogs that return to the home but won't allow approach: hide inside behind an open door, use food smell to entice the animal inside, then close the door.
stance_target: Passive environment-based capture is more effective than active pursuit for skittish returnees
scope_conditions: Dogs that have been sighted repeatedly near the escape point but flee when approached
quoted_support: hiding inside the home behind the open door, enticing the animal into the house with the smell of food, and slamming the door closed once the pet has entered the house.

claim: Intersection alerts (owner holds giant neon LOST DOG posters at street corners like sign twirlers) resulted in recovery for 14 of 43 families who used the technique in MARN's practice — a 33% success rate.
stance_target: High-visibility active marketing at intersections is an evidence-backed recovery technique
quoted_support: In the past, MARN instructed 43 different families to conduct intersection alerts and 14 of them got their lost dogs back by using this technique!

claim: The 2017 Missing Cat Study found 40% of lost cats recovered by physical search; median distance for outdoor-access cats was 17-house radius; median for escaped indoor-only cats was 2-house radius.
stance_target: Physical territory search is the primary recovery method and distance is breed/access-category dependent
scope_conditions: Cats specifically; Huang et al. 2018, Animals journal; data collected 2017
quoted_support: 40% of lost cats found by their guardians are recovered by conducting a physical search of the missing cat's territory. The median distance for outdoor-access cats that were lost and then found was a 17-house radius of their homes. For escaped indoor-only cats the median distance... was much closer — within a 2 house radius.

claim: Skittish dogs that become lost are difficult to recover primarily because multiple failed capture attempts by untrained rescuers escalate the dog's panic level before the owner can attempt calming techniques.
stance_target: Restricting who attempts capture is operationally critical — untrained approaches reduce recovery probability
scope_conditions: Skittish dogs after initial disappearance, especially in populated areas with active social media
quoted_support: Dogs with skittish temperaments that become lost are difficult to recover, primarily because they run from rescuers and often from their own guardians. By the time a guardian sees their skittish lost dog, it is probable that several would-be-rescuers already tried to capture him, sending the dog into a blind panic.
